narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've put into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our prediction model saw your path to the bar exam as riskier than we'd hoped—just over a 52% chance of not passing. Picture your profile coming together like chapters in a story: the strongest signal was your Year 3 decile of 3, which placed you in the lower third of your peers and whispered doubts about sustaining top performance under law school's mounting pressures. That echoed back to your Year 1 decile of 3, painting a picture of consistent challenges in keeping pace early on, which made the model worry about building momentum. Your LSAT score of 20 then stepped in as a big concern, signaling to the model that the analytical firepower needed for bar prep might be a stretch. Layered on top was your lower-middle family income, which the model linked to added hurdles like fewer resources for tutors or stress-free study time that often help others pull through. Your Asian background and female gender, while strengths in so many ways, aligned in the model's view with patterns where even high-achievers sometimes face steeper odds in this high-stakes arena. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.7 and full-time student status rounded out the tale, suggesting a foundation that might buckle without extra support. It's not that you're not capable—far from it—but together, these pieces formed a narrative of caution, one where the risks of bar failure edged out the promise. The good news? This is just one snapshot; with targeted steps like LSAT retakes or income-boosting scholarships, you could rewrite that story entirely. You've got the drive—keep pushing.
